title: practical english usage - Part 271
content:
or just Enjoy after serving a customer.

'grace' (a short prayer) before and after meals. Waiters often say Enjoy your meal That was lovely/delicious; thank you very much). Some religious people say Vocabulary Areas  Section 29 vocabulary •  330    meals Christmas/Thanksgiving dinner, even if they are eaten at midday.

evening meal. Celebration meals at Christmas and Thanksgiving are called Americans generally use lunch for the midday meal and dinner or supper for the 2 American      usage restaurant, is usually called dinner.

it is the main meal of the day. A  more formal evening meal with guests, or in a same word for a bedtime snack). Some people use dinner for the evening meal if A  meal later in the evening is often called supper (and some people use the d  later evening: supper or dinner tea or high tea; some people call it supper.

Many people have a cooked meal around five or six o’ clock. This is often called c   early evening: sometimes (high) tea or supper o’ clock in the afternoon) is now very unusual, though it is often served in hotels.

(Afternoon) tea (a light meal of tea with biscuits and cakes, taken at four or five b  afternoon: tea or packed meal.

Lunch is also the normal term used by most people for a light midday snack of the day. People who  are ‘ higher’  in the social scale usually call it lunch.

The  midday meal is often called dinner, especially if it is the main meal a  midday: dinner or lunch 1  British    usage There are regional and social differences in the names for meals.

we  may ask Did you sleep well? or How did you sleep?

When  somebody goes to bed, people often say Sleep well. In the morning, 20 sleep For more information about thanking and the use of please, ►    556.

small things.

Note that British people do not always reply to thanks, especially thanks for That's OK. (informal)No problem. (informal) Don't mention it.               That's (quite) all right.

Not at all. (formal)            You're welcome.

Possible replies to thanksare: Thanks. (informal)    Thank you so much.

Thank you very much.   Cheers. (informal BrE) not Thank you.       Thanks very much  / a lot. (     Thank you a-lot.) Common ways of thanking people are: 19 thanks vocabulary •  331    telephoning Hold the line, please.     Hang on. (informal) Hold on a moment, please. I'll just put you on hold.

Just a moment.        May I put you on hold?

8  asking     people     to  wait Could you Just tell her Jake called?

Could you ask her to ring/call me at/on 637022?

Could you ask her to ring/call me back?

I'll ring/call again later. (AmE  I'll call...) Please leave your message after the tone.

Can I leave a message?

Can I take a message?

I'm afraid she's not in at the moment.

7 if  somebody        is  not   there (AmE I'd like to make a collect call...) or I'd like to make a reverse (   transferred) charge call to 0449 437878.

6 if  you   want    the   other     person     to  pay   for   the   call How do I get an outside line?

What's the country code for Portugal?

What's the dialling code / area code for Bristol?

Can/Could I have extension two oh four six?

5 asking     for   a  number Who am I speaking to?       Who's calling please?

Who is that? (AmE Who is this?) Who is that speaking?

4 asking     who    somebody        is or This is Megan Horrabin (speaking).' 'Could I speak to Megan Horrabin? 'Speaking.' notusually Hello, this is Corinne. (                 ... I'm Corinne.) 3 saying     who    you   are Could I speak to Megan Horrabin? (AmE  also Could I speak with...?) 2 asking     for   a  person 'Albert Packard.' 'Hello.' answering a business phone most often give their name.

People answering a private phone either say Hello or give their name. People 1 answering        a  phone 331 telephoning Vocabulary Areas  Section 29 vocabulary •  332    idioms, collocations and formulaic expressions For details of phrasal and prepositional verbs, ►     12-13.

Continue as far as the crossroads. (formal) - Just keep on till you get to the crossroads. (informal) Please let us know when you plan to arrive. (more formal) - What time are you planning to turn up? (informal) writing. Compare: Many of these two-word verbs are especially common in informal speech and I gave up chemistry because I didn't like it.
